title: Heterotopic Ossification -- Staging
content: Staging is most often used for surgical planning and is not used much in rehabilitation management. Brooker Classification System, used for severity of HO in the hip, is the most commonly used staging system [8] , which uses plain film radiograph to evaluate the presence and size of bone deposition visually. A simplified version has been proposed called the "Della Valle" classification system [17] . None of the most commonly used rating systems take into account patient function or ROM, so a modified Brooker classification scale has been proposed that take these factors into account. Brooker classification divides the extent of HO formation in the hip into 4 classes:

title: Aneurysmal Bone Cysts -- Evaluation
content: Radiographs illustrate cystic or osteolytic lesions with thin “eggshell” sclerotic borders. The enclosed cavity contains many dividing septa. [2] The surrounding bone may be pushed outwards from its normal anatomy, demonstrating the aggressive, expansile nature of the lesion.

title: Aneurysmal Bone Cysts -- Treatment / Management
content: Intralesional excision is the preferred treatment of choice, which is similar to curettage: the surgeon makes a broad opening through the osseous wall of the lesion and removes the contents. This process allows a greater amount of the bone to remain intact to reduce patient morbidity when compared to en bloc excision. After the removal of the cystic contents, the surgeon can fill the lesion via bone grafting or other material to supply strength and promote healing of the bone. Differing from curettage, this type of treatment allows the use of various forms of adjuvant therapy to reduce the rates of recurrence, due to the broad opening made to gain access to the cavity. Adjuvant therapies include high-speed burr, argon beam coagulation, phenol, and cryotherapy. This type of excision is also beneficial in aneurysmal bone cysts that occur near joints and other structures where the desire to maintain normal anatomy is crucial for functionality.

Although not required for diagnosis, study results have demonstrated reduced BMD in the spine, hip, and forearm (see Image. Osteomalacia in Hip Joint, Radiograph). Iliac crest bone biopsy is considered the gold standard for establishing the diagnosis of osteomalacia. Due to this test's invasive and more aggressive nature, it should be reserved for when the diagnosis is in doubt, or the cause of osteomalacia is equivocal by any of the noninvasive methods. title: Osteopetrosis -- Histopathology
content: Histological evaluation of bone in a patient with osteopetrosis will likely show empty lacunae with plugged Haversian canals, calcified cartilage dispersed within bony trabeculae, and defective osteoclasts that lack a clear zone and ruffled border. These clear zone and ruffled border structures are the trademark findings in osteoclasts undergoing active resorption, and their absence corresponds perfectly with the findings of osteopetrosis. [10]

title: Osteopathic Manipulative Treatment: Counterstrain Procedure - Cervical Vertebrae -- Anatomy and Physiology
content: The cervical spine can be divided into the atlas (C1), the axis (C2), and the typical cervical vertebrae (C2-C7).  C1 has no spinous process or vertebral body. C2 has the dens that projects superiorly from its body and articulates with C1. As the union between the head and the atlas, the atlantooccipital (AO) joint's primary motion involves flexion and extension (nodding motion). The atlantoaxial (AA) joint's primary motion involves rotation. The vertebral bodies of the typical cervical segments are separated by intervertebral discs. [7] The upper typical cervicals (approximately C2-C4) have a primary motion of rotation. The lower typical cervicals (approximately C5-C7) have a primary motion of side-bending. The facet joints are synovial joints that display unique orientation along the cervical spine that aid in movement. Studies have determined that particular facet orientation has been correlated with clinical conditions. [8]

title: Blount Disease -- Treatment / Management -- Guided Growth
content: The most common procedure is hemiepiphysiodesis of the lateral epiphysis with extraperiosteal implants such as staples, pins, or tension band plating. Bone growth on the side of the growth plate where pinning or plating occurs stops or slows down, allowing the other side of the growth plate to grow normally and gradually straighten the bone over time. An advantage of this approach is that the entire physis can resume growth after hardware removal.

title: UMLS -- Run 23 -- Level 1 -- Community 209
content: The category “THIGH AND LEG: SURGICAL REPAIRS, CLOSURES AND RECONSTRUCTIONS” comprises a comprehensive set of operative interventions that target the femur and adjacent structures. Child procedures include epiphyseal arrest techniques—“Arrest, epiphyseal, any method (eg, epiphysiodesis); combined distal femur, proximal tibia and fibula,” “Arrest, epiphyseal, any method (eg, epiphysiodesis); distal femur,” and “Arrest, hemiepiphyseal, distal femur or proximal tibia or fibula (eg, genu varus or valgus)”—which modulate growth plates to correct deformities. Fracture‑stabilizing operations encompass “Femoral shortening with blade plate,” “Internal fixation of femur” (with and without fracture reduction), “Intramedullary nailing of femur,” “Open reduction of fracture of femur” (with or without internal fixation), and “Trochanterplasty.” Osteoplastic modifications are represented by “Osteoplasty of femur,” “Osteoplasty of femur, lengthening,” “Osteoplasty of femur, shortening,” and “Osteoplasty, femur; combined, lengthening and shortening with femoral segment transfer,” allowing alteration of femoral length and contour. Preventive and corrective measures include “Prophylactic treatment of femur,” “Repair of malunion, of femur, distal to head and neck,” and “Suture of periosteum of femur.” Prosthetic management is covered by “Replacement of prosthesis of femur with use of methyl methacrylate” and “Revision of total knee arthroplasty, all components (procedure).” Clinically, these procedures address the primary anatomical site of lower‑limb fractures, exemplified by the entity “fracture of lower limb” classified as an unspecified closed fracture of the femoral neck, underscoring the femur’s central role in diagnosis, fracture stabilization, growth modulation, length alteration, and implant revision within orthopedic care.

title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 2 -- Community 823
content: Osteoporosis/low bone mass (هشاشة العظام/قلة العظام (SMQ)) is a condition marked by diminished bone density that predisposes patients to skeletal fragility. Internal fixation of fractures (تثبيت داخلي للكسر), hip head replacement (إعاضة رأس الفخذ), and the occurrence of multiple fractures (كسور متعددة) are all recognized as clinical entities that belong to the osteoporosis/low bone mass category. These procedures and fracture patterns commonly arise because weakened bone is less able to withstand normal stresses, necessitating surgical stabilization or joint replacement. Effective management of osteoporosis/low bone mass therefore aims to strengthen bone, reduce the incidence of such fractures, and limit the need for invasive orthopedic interventions.

title: UMLS -- Run 7 -- Level 2 -- Community 755
content: The category “THIGH AND LEG: SURGICAL REPAIRS, CLOSURES AND RECONSTRUCTIONS” is a subcategory of “treatment; fracture or dislocation of lower extremity (other than hip or femur)” and encompasses a range of operative interventions focused on the femur, proximal tibia, fibula, and trochanteric region. Specific procedures classified as children of this category include “Arrest, epiphyseal, any method (eg, epiphysiodesis); distal femur,” which addresses growth‑plate modulation of the distal femur, and “Arrest, hemiepiphyseal, distal femur or proximal tibia or fibula (eg, genu varus or valgus),” which corrects angular deformities of the lower limb by partially arresting growth. The category also contains “Internal fixation of femur” and its variant “Internal fixation of femur without fracture reduction,” both of which provide stable fixation for femoral fractures, with the latter used when anatomical reduction is not achieved. Bone‑shaping and lengthening techniques are represented by “Osteoplasty of femur,” “Osteoplasty of femur, lengthening,” and the more complex “Osteoplasty, femur; combined, lengthening and shortening with femoral segment transfer,” which allow for correction of limb length discrepancies and deformities through controlled osteotomy and segment manipulation. Management of post‑traumatic deformity is addressed by “Repair of malunion, of femur, distal to head and neck,” which restores proper alignment of the femur distal to the proximal femoral region. Additionally, “Trochanterplasty” is listed as a child procedure, indicating surgical modification of the greater trochanter for indications such as hip abductor insufficiency or prosthetic fitting. Collectively, these procedures illustrate the comprehensive surgical toolkit available for addressing fractures, dislocations, growth‑plate disorders, angular deformities, malunions, and length discrepancies in the thigh and leg, thereby supporting optimal functional recovery and limb alignment.

title: UMLS -- Run 17 -- Level 3 -- Community 2179
content: Osteoporosis/low bone mass (SMQ) includes several fracture‑related conditions and orthopedic interventions. Internal fixation of fractures (تثبيت داخلي للكسر) is a member of the osteoporosis/low bone mass category, reflecting that patients with reduced bone density often require surgical stabilization of broken bones. Hip head replacement (إعاضة رأس الفخذ) also belongs to this category, indicating that severe osteoporotic changes can lead to hip joint damage that necessitates prosthetic replacement. In addition, other fractures are listed as members of osteoporosis/low bone mass, underscoring the broad susceptibility of osteoporotic individuals to multiple fracture types. Recognizing these relationships helps clinicians anticipate and manage orthopedic needs in patients with osteoporosis or low bone mass.
